title: Feed-Forward Networks in Transformers: Architecture, Parameters & Efficiency - Interactive | Michael Brenndoerfer | Michael Brenndoerfer
snippet: The position-wise feed-forward network was introduced in the original transformer paper "Attention Is All You Need" by Vaswani et al. (2017). The authors used a two-layer architecture with ReLU activation and a 4x hidden dimension expansion factor, choices that have become de facto standards across the field. What started as a practical design decision, pairing a nonlinear sublayer with the attention sublayer, turned out to encode deep inductive biases about how information should be processed in language models. Subsequent research has refined the activation function (ReLU to GELU to SiLU/Swish), explored gated variants (GLU, SwiGLU), and investigated the FFN's role as associative memory, but the basic two-layer expand-and-contract structure has remained remarkably stable across nearly a [...] class FeedForwardNetwork: class  FeedForwardNetwork:  """  """ Position-wise feed-forward network for transformer blocks. Position-wise feed-forward network for transformer blocks.  Implements: FFN(x) = activation(x @ W1 + b1) @ W2 + b2 Implements: FFN(x) = activation(x @ W1 + b1) @ W2 + b2  """  """  def __init__(self, d_model, d_ff, activation=relu, use_bias=True):  def  __init__(self, d_model, d_ff, activation =relu, use_bias = True):  """  """ Initialize the feed-forward network. Initialize the feed-forward network.  Args: Args: d_model: Input and output dimension d_model: Input and output dimension d_ff: Hidden dimension (typically 4  d_model) d_ff: Hidden dimension (typically 4  d_model) activation: Nonlinear activation function activation: Nonlinear activation function use_bias: [...] Two-layer architecture: The FFN formula FFN(x)=œÉ(xW1‚Äã+b1‚Äã)W2‚Äã+b2‚Äã consists of an expansion layer (dmodel‚Äã‚Üídff‚Äã), a nonlinear activation œÉ (ReLU, GELU, etc.), and a contraction layer (dff‚Äã‚Üídmodel‚Äã). The standard expansion factor is dff‚Äã=4√ódmodel‚Äã, establishing a working space four times larger than the model dimension where nonlinear transformations are easier to learn.

title: Position-wise Feed-Forward Networks
snippet:
Position-wise Feed-Forward Networks

# Position-wise Feed-Forward Networks

Position-wise Feed-Forward Networks (FFNs) are important components within each encoder and decoder layer. These networks process vectors, adding further processing capacity to the model.

Despite its name, it's actually a very simple network. It consists of two linear transformations with a Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation function in between. The "position-wise" part is significant: this same feed-forward network is applied independently and identically to each position (i.e., each token's representation) in the sequence. [...] CS224N: Natural Language Processing with Deep Learning - Lecture 12: Transformers and Pretraining, Stanford University, 2023 - Lecture slides from a leading university course, providing a structured explanation of the Transformer architecture and its components, including the FFN. [...] FFN(x)=ReLU(xW1‚Äã+b1‚Äã)W2‚Äã+b2‚Äã

Where:

 x is the output from the preceding layer (the Add & Norm layer following the attention mechanism) for a specific position.
 W1‚Äã and b1‚Äã are the weight matrix and bias term for the first linear transformation.
 W2‚Äã and b2‚Äã are the weight matrix and bias term for the second linear transformation.
 ReLU is the Rectified Linear Unit activation function, defined as ReLU(z)=max(0,z).

The dimensionality typically changes within the FFN. The input and output dimension is dmodel‚Äã (the model's embedding dimension), but the inner layer dimension, often denoted as dff‚Äã, is usually larger. A common configuration, as used in the original Transformer paper, is dff‚Äã=4√ódmodel‚Äã. For example, if dmodel‚Äã=512, then dff‚Äã=2048.
